title: Are We There Yet? Assessing the Readiness of Single-Cell ...
text:
Single-cell analysis is an active area of research in many fields of biology. Measurements at single-cell resolution allow researchers to study diverse populations without losing biologically meaningful information to sample averages. Many technologies have been used to study single cells, including mass spectrometry-based single-cell proteomics (SCP). SCP has seen a lot of growth over the past couple of years through improvements in data acquisition and analysis, leading to greater proteomic depth. Because method development has been the main focus in SCP, biological applications have been sprinkled in only as proof-of-concept. However, SCP methods now provide significant coverage of the proteome and have been implemented in many laboratories. Thus, a primary question to address in our community is whether the current state of technology is ready for widespread adoption for biological inquiry. In this Perspective, we examine the potential for SCP in three thematic areas of biological investigation: cell annotation, developmental trajectories, and spatial mapping. We identify that the primary limitation of SCP is sample throughput. As proteome depth has been the primary target for method development to date, we advocate for a change in focus to facilitate measuring tens of thousands of single-cell proteomes to enable biological applications beyond proof-of-concept.
Are We There Yet? Assessing the Readiness of Single-Cell Proteomics to Answer Biological Hypotheses | Journal of Proteome Research

Abstract
--------

Click to copy section linkSection link copied!

!

High Resolution Image

Download MS PowerPoint Slide

Single-cell analysis is an active area of research in many fields of biology. Measurements at single-cell resolution allow researchers to study diverse populations without losing biologically meaningful information to sample averages. Many technologies have been used to study single cells, including mass spectrometry-based single-cell proteomics (SCP). SCP has seen a lot of growth over the past couple of years through improvements in data acquisition and analysis, leading to greater proteomic depth. Because method development has been the main focus in SCP, biological applications have been sprinkled in only as proof-of-concept. However, SCP methods now provide significant coverage of the proteome and have been implemented in many laboratories. Thus, a primary question to address in our community is whether the current state of technology is ready for widespread adoption for biological inquiry. In this Perspective, we examine the potential for SCP in three thematic areas of biological investigation: cell annotation, developmental trajectories, and spatial mapping. We identify that the primary limitation of SCP is sample throughput. As proteome depth has been the primary target for method development to date, we advocate for a change in focus to facilitate measuring tens of thousands of single-cell proteomes to enable biological applications beyond proof-of-concept.

#### SPECIAL ISSUE

This article is part of the Single-Cell Omics special issue.

Introduction
------------

Click to copy section linkSection link copied!

---

Biology begins at the level of single cells, thus a wide variety of technologies have been developed to visualize and characterize individual cells. (1−3);) Technological limitations sometimes require the pooled analysis of many cells, called a bulk sample, but this generates data based on population averages, which can obscure the individuality of cells, the heterogeneity of a sample, the differences in cell-type abundances, and the complex interactions between cells that are the heart of biology. (4,5);)

Over the years, many technologies for studying single cells have been developed, beginning with the light microscope and Leeuwenhoek’s discovery of cells. Beyond the gross morphology and large organelles, stains and antibodies were developed to highlight and track specific molecules, including DNA, RNA, proteins, and other macromolecular components. Stains can be combined with a variety of handling and visualization technologies, including: fluorescent imaging (6,7);) and cytometry. (8−10);) Yet, methods that target specific molecules only provide a small picture of what is going on within a cell, leading to a desire for unbiased measurements. Single-cell sequencing methods provide a more comprehensive characterization of DNA or RNA, allowing researchers to learn about gene expression, gene regulation, and genetic variability of individual single cells. (11−17);)

As proteins provide the vast majority of cellular functions for metabolism, environmental interaction, signaling, and structure, there is a need for the direct measurement of proteins within single cells. (18,19);) Sequencing technologies are unreliable in approximating protein abundances. (20,21);) Thus, mass spectrometry-based single-cell proteomics (MS-based SCP) has rapidly evolved to provide these critical measurements. (22,23);) The work in SCP to date has been dominated by workflow and method development. (24−29);) The number of protein identifications and quantifications have significantly increased in the last year, with methods now achieving over 3,000 proteins per single cell. (28,30);) Thus, we believe that SCP will soon transition to biological applications.

When evaluating whether SCP is ready as a technology to drive biological discovery, we first evaluated how single-cell analyses are being used in discovery-based research. As a broad field, single-cell transcriptomics is used in thousands of projects. Although each paper has its own unique hypotheses and experimental goals, a common core of many of these experiments can be simplified down to one of three fundamental investigations: cell annotation, developmental trajectories, and spatial mapping. As proteins are the functional molecules, single-cell proteomics has arisen as an alternative method for characterizing cellular state. Although not yet routine, we anticipate that single-cell mass spectrometry may soon yield broader measurements of post-translational modifications, providing insight into regulatory activities in, and function and heterogeneity of, cells through protein activity. In transitioning hypotheses from single-cell transcriptomics to single-cell proteomics, the three fundamental experimental designs (cell annotation, developmental trajectories, and spatial mapping) will also be essential. Therefore, our goal is to investigate whether SCP can adequately produce data allowing researchers to use these fundamental experimental designs, what roadblocks and considerations need to be resolved before employing each method on proteomic data.

Materials and Methods
---------------------

Click to copy section linkSection link copied!

---

All code used for calculations and figures can be found at <https://github.com/PayneLab/WhySingleCell>.

### Cell Annotation ─ Number of Cells

The following math and rationale was used to calculate the baseline number of cells required for an annotation experiment.

### Consideration 1: Random Sampling from a Population

Imagine a population of white blood cells that contains the five main subtypes at the following concentrations: neutrophils (62% of the sample), eosinophils (2.3% of the sample), basophils (0.4% of the sample), lymphocytes (30% of the sample), and monocytes (5.3% of the sample). Let us also imagine that in order to feel confident that you characterized a basophil, you would need to see at least five basophils in your results. How many cells would you need to sample and measure to have a 99% chance of sampling each cell type in your sample at least 5 times? Assuming that you sample and measure cells at random from your population and that your sample is large enough that measuring a cell is akin to sampling with replacement (“infinite” population), this problem can be simplified into one that fits a binomial distribution. The chance of success (or sampling the rarest subtype) is 0.4% and the chance of failure (or not sampling the rarest subtype) is 99.6% for each trial (or each cell sampled and measured). If *n* represents the total number of cells sampled, *x* represents the number of successes, *k* represents the minimum number of successes required, *p* represents the probability of a success event, and *P* represents the overall probability, we can represent this problem with the following equation:

P=∑x=kn(xn)px(1−p)n−x

By setting *P* = 0.99, *p* = 0.04, and *k* = 5 and solving for *n*, we can determine that 3,274 cells would need to be sampled and measured to have a 99% probability of sampling the rarest cell type (and therefore all other subtypes) at least 5 times.

### Consideration 2: Sample Dropout

Finally, imagine that not every cell you measure has reliable or usable output and that the expected dropout rate for our samples is 1%. This means that the number of cells sampled needs to be adjusted up in order to account for the sample dropout. If *n* represents the number of cells we need to measure, *d* represents the dropout rate, and *n**adj* represents the dropout-adjusted number of cells we need to sample, we can adjust the number of cells we need to sample with the following equation:

nadj=ceil(n1−d)

Given the infinite population described above, and thereby setting *n* = 3274, and setting *d* = 0.01, and solving for *n**adj*, we can determine that 3,308 cells would need to be sampled and measured to have a 99% probability of sampling the rarest cell type (and therefore all other subtypes) at least 5 times in an “infinite” population, adjusting for sample dropout.

Cell Annotation
---------------

Click to copy section linkSection link copied!

---

### What is Cell Annotation?

An initial step in many biological experiments is to determine cell identity, answering such questions as, “what cells are in my sample?” or “does my sample contain diseased cells?” Answering these questions is done by annotating, or labeling, the samples. The most common ways that cells are disassociated from tissues and collected for single-cell analyses result in cells that are often unlabeled and need to be annotated for further downstream analyses, such as differential expression. (For example, a blood draw collects many kinds of cells. Individual cells sorted from a blood draw do not have a name tag specifying them as “B cells”. Thus, their identity has to be inferred from the data.) A critical driver in analysis methods for cell annotation is whether there is a cell atlas containing profiles of various cell types for matching. (31);) Cell atlases provide a convenient reference of expression profiles for specific cell types which are often used to train machine learning models to classify the samples. (32);) Without an atlas, cell annotation is typically performed via clustering within a single data set. Cluster labels are then determined from canonical markers. (33−36);)

### Historical Look at Cell Annotation

Cell type annotation has been done for decades with antibodies and stains, for example the extensive work in immunology to uncover the numerous types of white blood cells and their various activated states. (37);) The depth of molecular characterization offered by proteomics and RNA sequencing ushered in a more comprehensive description of cellular state. However, as mentioned above, using bulk samples leads to sample averages in molecular measurements, thus biasing against rare cell types within a population. (1);) Single-cell annotation analyses allow us to overcome these biases and identify cellular subpopulations, including rare subpopulations, (19,34,38);) and provide a more comprehensive view of the cell. (33,34,39,40);) Single-cell annotation has often revealed new cell types (39,41);) and subtypes. (36,42);)

### SCP and Cell Annotation

Cell annotation is used in single-cell proteomics, but most often as a validation of method development to demonstrate the ability to separate a few distinct cell types. (43,44);) In single-cell transcriptomics experiments, cell annotation is often the first of many steps in data analysis; (45,46);) yet in proteomics, it is typically limited to proof-of-concept applications rather than biological hypothesis-based applications. Below we explore potential limitations of SCP data and analysis techniques that prevent a broader application of cell annotation.

### Considerations for SCP in Cell Annotation

To provide meaningful annotations for downstream analyses, SCP requires sufficient sample throughput and proteomic depth. (25);) Additionally, to make the best use of cell annotation in an SCP experiment, several considerations must be made when analyzing the data so that common pitfalls are avoided and results are as accurate as possible. We outline four important considerations: (1) the number of cells sampled and measured, (2) the depth of the proteome measured in each cell, (3) integrating across experiments and overcoming batch effects, and (4) double-dipping during data analysis.

### Number of Cells

The number of cells an SCP experiment needs to analyze depends on many parameters, but some foundational considerations include: (1) the expected rarity/concentration of the desired cell types and (2) the expected sample dropout/viability rate. Additionally, the number of cells could depend on the method for annotation, e.g. whether or not a reference atlas is used.

We demonstrate how to estimate the number of cells that need to be sampled using an example of the main subtypes of white blood cells: neutrophils (62%), lymphocytes (30%), monocytes (5.3%), eosinophils (2.3%), and basophils (0.4%). To feel confident that the rarest subtype was minimally characterized, we set a goal of sampling at least five basophils. Assuming that cells are sampled at random from the population, 3,274 cells are required to have a 99% probability of sampling the rarest cell type at least 5 times (see Methods). To plan for failed data acquisitions, we assume that about 1% of the cells sampled will be removed in quality control/assurance. Therefore, the number of cells needing to be sampled can be adjusted to account for the sample dropout - here up to 3,308 cells. To obtain a more complete characterization of intracell-type diversity for rare cells, a significantly higher number of cells would need to be sampled in this example.

The calculated numbers above are for one sample (single time point of one person). Proper experimental design often includes biological replicates and multiple individuals, disease states, time points, etc. Thus, the total number of single cells necessary for an experiment would certainly be at least tens of thousands. We also note that if the desire is to build a reference atlas of single-cell profiles across diverse cell types - an extremely valuable resource to accelerate science - a large number of individuals would be required, pushing the number of cells measured toward hundreds of thousands or millions. Currently, the most cells processed in one SCP experiment is ∼3,000. (25);) Thus, data acquisition throughput poses a major challenge for cell-type annotation in SCP.

### Proteomic Depth

The question of needed proteomic depth is not as easily calculated and will depend on the cell types examined as well as the abundance of the distinguishing proteins. Currently, single-cell proteomic methods generally measure the more abundant proteins within a cell. As depth has increased in SCP, more of the medium-abundant proteins have been captured; however, few low-abundant proteins are measured. (28);) The most common type of cell-type annotation utilizes a broad set of markers, often called a profile. With data from the Human Proteome Map, (47);) we examined proteins which were determined to be specific and/or enriched for tissues. These proteins are medium to high abundance, but many are required to accurately differentiate between similar tissues. Since these proteins are stochastically sampled in a SCP experiment, even for highly abundant proteins, sufficient depth is required to sample enough of the proteins in the profile for an accurate annotation of the sample. A second type of cell annotation is sometimes performed with a single protein or gene’s presence, e.g a specific subtype like CD4+ T cells. Here the presence of CD4 is used to distinguish a subtype of T cells which have already been classified by their broader profile. For the narrow subtype annotation, only a single measurement matters. However, in a global and unbiased assay, measuring the right protein may be challenging if it is low abundance. With the current publicized efforts in SCP methodology attaining 3,000+ proteins, we believe that most cell annotation exercises are likely to be successful. The depth of SCP coverage is on par with, and sometimes exceeds, that of scRNA-Seq experiments, depending on technology platform, cell type, etc.

### Batch Effects

Underlying many common data analysis techniques (like cell annotation and differential expression) is the assumption that abundance measurements can be compared between samples. Batch effects, one type of technical noise, introduce unwanted variation into the protein abundance measurements, making them not directly comparable across samples. This can mask biological variation or over exaggerate its importance and reduce statistical power in downstream analyses. (48);) Batch effects occur when samples are not appropriately randomized across batches. This randomization needs to occur for sample types (such as case and control samples) as well as for cell types. When this is not done, the data is processed in unbalanced batches and things like different methods of sample preparation or instrument acquisition can introduce substantial changes to the resulting MS data, (49,50);) making it difficult to successfully annotate cells based on markers or profiles derived in different laboratories. In SCP, batch effects are more common because researchers are more often working across large data-acquisition time scales, creating more opportunities for instrument drift and operator differences. A second substantial source of batch effects comes from trying to join data sets across laboratories, such as metaanalyses or using a reference atlas for cell annotation. In these scenarios, batch effects are unavoidable, and often large, especially as SCP is still evolving in sample preparation and sample acquisition strategies.

The most reliable method to correct for batch effects is via calibration of the abundance measurements between samples using either internal or external standards. (51,52);) Unfortunately, this is rare in practice; while spike-in standards are common for clinical proteomics, we have not yet observed them in any SCP experimental designs. It may be tempting to simply correct for batch effects using an analysis tool after data acquisition. However, incomplete or incorrect removal of batch effects through these tools is common, especially in experiments with poor or unbalanced designs (such as combining data sets from multiple experiments) and incorrect implementations of the correction methods and tools. (48,53);) Experimental design in single-cell proteomics should plan on the use of a calibration standard for measurement comparability across samples.

### Double-Dipping

An important consideration to make when analyzing the results of cell-type annotation is the concept of double-dipping, which happens when the same data used for creating the clusters is later used for testing for differential expression. (54,55);) Sometimes double-dipping is referred to as double use of data or circular analyses. In this scenario, the discriminating proteins are defining features of clusters and therefore are obviously and by definition differentially abundant, this circularity nullifies the results of differential expression - by which we mean that the produced p-values are inaccurate and untrustworthy. (56−58);) A second source of double-dipping can be found when classifiers are trained from large atlases. Many workflows appear to leak testing data into their training data by using the whole data set for feature selection prior to training (best practices require that training and testing data be separated prior to any work, including feature selection). (59);) We also note that using a PCA or transformed matrix for clustering and then an untransformed matrix for differential statistics does not remove the double-dipping. Unfortunately, double-dipping is a very common problem in single-cell processing workflows. (54,60);)

While double-dipping is a problem, there are ways to overcome it. To create models that are not overfit to the reference data, it is important to make sure the reference data set is large enough to be split into two parts, or that two reference data sets are used - one is used for feature selection and the other is used to train and test the classification model. (59);) To annotate the data in a way that still allows for downstream analyses, “top-scoring pairs” (TSP) can be used. (61);) The TSP method uses relationships between two measurements within a sample to train models and predict classifications (for example, protein\_1 < protein\_2). This method provides two main benefits. First, it does not apply classifications using the protein abundances, meaning that later analyses relying on those measurements can be used without compromising the significance of the p-values. Second, the relationships used in the classifier provide interpretable biological information that can become the hypotheses for future experiments. (61);) As SCP moves toward biological investigation through cell annotation, members of the community need to be aware of the problem of double-dipping and how to overcome it in their experiments to produce the best results from their data.

Developmental Trajectories
--------------------------

Click to copy section linkSection link copied!

---

### What are Developmental Trajectories?

Cells gain an identity through differentiation and development. The above discussion of cell annotation presupposes that cellular states are discrete and well-defined; however, cells undergoing development may be observed in a continuous distribution of intermediate stages. By definition, cell annotation does not allow for a gradual arc of progression. The goal of trajectory inference is to identify what molecules are changing over time and identify the biological process associated with those changes. (62);) This is done by ordering samples along a pseudotime trajectory, such that changes between neighboring cells are minimized. (60,63,64);) The results can be visualized by overlaying the data points and the inferred trajectory in a reduced dimensionality projection such as a UMAP. (60);)

### Historical Look at Developmental Trajectories

Trajectory inference analysis methods have been motivated by biological questions surrounding cellular differentiation, immune response, disease progression, cell-fate prediction, and treatment response. (65,66);) Through trajectory inference analyses, regulators related to myoblast cell differentiation and molecular cascades involved in neurogenesis initiation have been discovered. (67,68);) Further, developmental trajectory analyses have identified key features in cellular development, such as early B cell development. (69);) While great strides have been made in investigating biology through trajectory inference, this is most often through scRNA-Seq Yet, transcript dynamics do not always indicate changes to protein abundances, especially within single cells, suggesting that SCP could add valuable insights to developmental trajectories. (70,71);)

### SCP and Developmental Trajectories

While SCP analyses are able to detect changes in proteins across a trajectory and elucidate developmental cascades, the field is only in the beginning stages. In 2019, Zhu et al. used trajectory analysis of differentiating hair cells in embryonic chicks to better understand the acquisition of hearing and balance. (72);) In 2021, Schoof et al. reconstructed developmental trajectories for blasts, progenitors, and leukemia stem cells, exemplifying the potential for SCP methods to discover new developmental mechanisms. (25);) In 2024, Guise et al. investigated proteome dynamics in the trajectory of dying neurons in ALS patients by characterizing single motor neurons from post-mortem spinal cords with a varying degree of TDP43 plaque buildup. (73);)

### Considerations for SCP in Developmental Trajectories

In order for SCP to be used for developmental trajectories, it requires sufficient sample throughput, appropriate analysis tools, and accurate data analysis. Below, three important considerations for SCP data in developmental trajectory inference analysis are outlined: (1) the number of cells sampled and measured, (2) availability of trajectory inference tools for SCP, and (3) double-dipping in the data.

### Number of Cells

There are two options for acquiring cells for a trajectory-based biological process. Unlike imaging technologies, mass spectrometry requires the destruction of the sample, making it impossible to follow the same individual cell through its stages of progression. If all stages of the trajectory are present at the same time in a sample (e.g., hematopoietic cell development in a bone marrow sample), then sampling many cells in a population can capture all states of development. (68,69,74);) However, some biological processes, like disease progression, happen over a period of time, making it impossible to capture the full developmental trajectory with a single snapshot of data; thus, repeated measurements are required to construct a full trajectory. (66,67);)

The exact number of cells needed will depend on the complexity of the trajectory being constructed, how many transition states there are, and the amount of time a cell spends in each state. Sampling strategies must consider ways to capture cell diversity within a single moment of development, along the trajectory, and between biological replicates (Figure 1). (62,75);) With the more limited throughput of SCP, managing these three elements in the experimental design will call for trade-offs between the number of cells per trajectory and the number of replicate trajectories. We note that it may be necessary to employ nonrandom sampling in order to appropriately acquire the right mix of cells. (73);) Optimal designs are experiment dependent, but Boekweg et al. provide a framework for trajectory-based power analyses and simulating the true positive and false positive discovery rates for a specific experimental design. (66);)

Figure 1
--------

!

Figure 1. Balancing the number of samples per replicate and the number of replicates in developmental trajectory experiments. Due to the limited throughput capabilities of SCP, experimental designs may need to decide between the number of samples per inferred trajectory and the number of replicate trajectories. In the example above, one 200-sample trajectory could be made (a), or four 50-sample trajectories could be made (b). Having multiple biological replicates helps to confirm the inferred structure of the trajectory and that the trajectory holds across multiple subjects, though fewer samples along the trajectory may provide a less accurate inferred structure in some cases.

### Trajectory Inference Analysis Tools

Current trajectory inference tools were originally created for scRNA-Seq, (60,65);) though some tools advertise their utility on many different data types. (68);) While some of these transcriptomics tools have been used in SCP, (25,72);) it is important to ensure that the tool does not implicitly assume RNA data. For example, to add directionality onto a trajectory map, some software tools will use the presence of incompletely spliced transcripts. (64,76);) Common proteomics pipelines do not explicitly model “immature” versions of proteins. Therefore, caution should be taken when repurposing tools specifically created for RNA measurements.

### Double-Dipping

To create a trajectory, cells are explicitly ordered by minimum expression changes, forming a pseudotime axis. After the creation of a trajectory, subsequent analyses often involve differential expression to identify changes along the trajectory. (60,77);) As discussed above, using data to order or group samples and then to identify differential abundance between groups is double-dipping; thus, any p-values obtained through these analyses are no longer statistically accurate or meaningful. (65);) Within trajectory analysis, double-dipping can be avoided if an external marker is used for creating the pseudotime, as was done by Guise et al. where TDP43 staining accompanied single-cell proteomics. (73);) If double-dipping in the data cannot be avoided, it should be acknowledged when findings are presented and conclusions should not be based on circular analyses. (78);)

Spatial Mapping
---------------

Click to copy section linkSection link copied!

---

### What is Spatial Mapping?

One last method garnering excitement is spatial mapping, which refers to a family of techniques that place molecular measurements of cells within their native spatial organization, e.g. cell location within tissues. This perspective has been essential in understanding intercellular interactions, such as in embryo development, (79);) liver regeneration, (80);) and tumor invasion and the creation of a suitable microenvironment. (81,82);)

### Historical Look at Spatial Mapping

Spatial mapping is the oldest single-cell technology. By combining microscopy with various stains, scientists have been able to view the spatial organization of heterogeneous cells for over 100 years. (83,84);) Originally, these stains were obtained from natural sources and had limited specificity. With the advent of molecular biology, several methods facilitated quantitative measurement of nucleotides and proteins in single cells. In situ hybridization, and later protein tagging techniques like GFP-cloning, both visualize gene/protein targets within cells. (85,86);) Ultimately, these methods are limited to specific, predetermined targets. Unbiased molecular measurements within cells emerged with MALDI-MS imaging, (87);) by taking mass spectra of tissue “pixels” and rastering over the entire sample. Within the past decade, the rapid improvements in proteomics and transcriptomics technologies have both been applied to spatial mapping. (88−90);) Although spatial resolution is a variable parameter, the size of a pixel is typically more than a single cell for both proteomics (91);) and transcriptomics. (92);)

### SCP and Spatial Mapping

Two common experimental designs are currently employed for spatial proteomics: MALDI and laser capture microdissection. With both techniques, there are trade-offs between speed of data acquisition and proteomic depth of discovery (Figure 2). MALDI has a long history with mass spectrometry imaging, (93,94);) but suffers from low sensitivity when used at high spatial resolutions. (95);) One challenge with MALDI-MS is the difficulty with obtaining MS2 information for a large number of ions. In practicality, these measurements are limited to MS1 measurements of most precursors. This significantly limits the depth of proteome characterization. However, MALDI can raster over a large tissue slice much quicker than laser capture techniques.

High Resolution Image

Download MS PowerPoint Slide

Laser capture microdissection coupled with mass spectrometry (LCM-MS) involves manually cutting out individual regions of interest from a sample using a laser and then introducing these to the proteomics workflow. This technique suffers from low throughput due to the time and expertise often required to isolate regions of interest, but enjoys high sensitivity. (91,96−98);) An additional advantage of LCM-MS is that the laser can cut any region of interest, including exact borders of individual cells (26,90);) or subcellular locations. (99);) Cellular material from LCM can easily be integrated into standard liquid chromatography separations, thus allowing for a deep interrogation of the sample.

### Considerations for SCP in Spatial Mapping

As SCP moves toward biological discovery through spatial mapping, it is important to consider the trade-offs between different spatial experimental designs. Below, these two important considerations for SCP data in spatial mapping analyses are outlined: (1) the resolution of samples, and (2) the number of samples acquired during the experiment.

### Resolution

When spatially analyzing a slice of tissue, an important decision in the experimental design is the spatial resolution. Using LCM, it is possible to cut out a single cell, (26,73);) or to cut out larger pixels of an arbitrary shape and size. (91,97,98);) Larger pixels will contain more peptides and increase MS signal. This is important as the number of peptide identifications in SCP is heavily determined by the amount of peptide material. The difference between 1 and 10 cells, or between a neuron and an epithelial cell, can significantly change the number of identifications. However, larger pixels will not truly be single cell measurements, which could obscure or blur out important information. Time is a primary concern for LCM experiments, as each laser captured region takes time to cut the tissue free; using simple shapes like squares can be fairly rapid, but manually tracing cell outlines is time-consuming.

### Number of Samples

In SCP, the number of samples (pixels) acquired is also an important experimental design parameter. In spatial transcriptomics, commercially available platforms have a standardized spatial resolution and geometry, e.g. the 10X Visium platform contains 5,000 spots per slide. However, in spatial proteomics, there are no commercial platforms and experiments are designed as fit-for-purpose. With the current throughput of SCP at a few hundred samples per day, (24,100,101);) it is not realistic to analyze 5,000 pixels \* tissue types \* biological replicates. Analyzing only one tissue slice with 5,000 pixels would take nearly a month of instrument time.

As data acquisition throughput is a significant bottleneck, there are several ways to decrease the number of samples in a spatial context. Decreasing the pixel resolution (increasing the pixel size and the number of cells per pixel) would allow for faster scanning of the tissue, but could be detrimental if true single-cell resolution is necessary. The deep-visual proteomics (DVP) workflow offers the ability to assay many cells and cells of the same type, but still suffers from the primary limitation that those cells or groups of cells need to be cut out of the tissue and placed in a well for sample preparation one at a time. (26);) In these workflows, one cell or group vs 10,000 cells or groups is a 10,000 fold difference in time. (If DVP is used to pool cells of the same cell type, it is no longer single cell and cannot answer specific questions about intercellular heterogeneity). If high resolution cannot be compromised, it is possible to analyze fewer spots by exclusively analyzing regions of interest instead of a complete rastering of the tissue. This approach is amenable to specific hypotheses, but not a global discovery experiment. Because proteomic spatial mapping faces trade-offs between resolution and the number of samples being analyzed, researchers must ask what data is required to answer their question given specific practical, financial, and time constraints before beginning an experiment.

Conclusion
----------

Click to copy section linkSection link copied!

---

Single-cell biology has exploded in the past decade, with a wide variety of methods offering an increasingly detailed characterization of DNA, RNA and proteins. Mass spectrometry-based single-cell proteomics has great potential in investigating biology, adding an essential perspective for discovery. Much of the work to date in MS-based SCP experiments has been to improve data acquisition and analysis methods. In light of these substantial improvements, we report that proteome depth does not appear to be a limiting factor in addressing many biological hypotheses. We also note many new computational-focused manuscripts which highlight challenges and potential solutions to data analysis tasks for single-cell data. (102−107);) This perspective covers three experimental designs used in biological applications that are common in single-cell biology and examines whether SCP is ready.

Given the dramatic increase in data volume and complexity, experimental design needs to be a major consideration in SC
